entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
Students will explore characteristics shared by all Image-Bearers and learn how to guard against good servants becoming bad masters by continuing Charlotte Mason's text written directly to students. Students finish Book I of Ourselves by the end of the course.

This topic is included in the following course(s): Citizenship: Grade 9 (U.S.), Citizenship: Grade 9 (Canada)

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content: Ourselves by Charlotte Mason is scheduled over five years. In a multi-age context, students can jump into the book wherever the class is reading. Otherwise, students should begin the book in Part 1.

Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

blocks.text:
Teachers may find it helpful to pre-read all or part of the selection from Plutarch studied and choose which news sites to offer students.

If you, the student, are learning at home, work to schedule a time with your teacher when you can complete the lessons from Ourselves and Plutarch together, as these lessons are much richer when shared and discussed.
Connect back to the previous lesson. (Optional for Current Events).

For news sources: Scan the headlines first to get an overview, and then choose a story to read. 

For Citizenship books (optional): Use a picture, a map, or an engaging question to get yourselves interested in the lesson. If necessary, you may talk about one or two unfamiliar words that will be crucial to understanding. It is not necessary to cover all unfamiliar words.

Read the selection assigned or the news story chosen. For rich, dense, or more challenging books such as Ourselves and Plutarch, it is a good idea to read them aloud. Have students keep an eye out for things to add to their Citizenship Notebook, Book of Centuries, and/or Commonplace book.

Share your thoughts on the story and any connections made. For current events, look up where events occurred on a map and ask questions together to discern the quality of the piece. (e.g., Are there sources cited? Is there an obvious bias? If so, what do other authors have to say on this subject? Are arguments supported by evidence? Is that evidence credible?) Consider reading an alternative perspective.
Record important ideas in your Citizenship Notebooks. Make a calendar of current events.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
Ch.XII p.136-137 "We must know" - "govern our actions."

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Choose a habit you would like to cultivate and tell a little about why. Draw a chart for keeping up with your new habit and check in daily. See example in Citizenship Notebook PDF in Quick Links. 

➜ DISCUSS (with remaining time)
Write or tell about the impact that governing our actions can have upon those around us.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
Ch.XII p.137-139 "I must be Just" - "as they are."

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log. Start a chart where you can list desirable human character traits on one side and their daemons on the other.

➜ JOURNAL (with remaining time)
Write of a time you were able to pay the dues of justice by thinking fairly, speaking truly, or acting justly toward someone else.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
Ch.XIII p.140-143 "We begin to" - "are gently received."

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• COMPOSITION -  Persuasive
Write at least two paragraphs to persuade your peers to be thoughtful and considerate, avoiding injuring body and soul of others.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 4
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
Ch.XIII p.143-145 "We are not" - "he belongs to."

• Think of a time someone was disagreeable.  How did or could you have used candour to see the other side.

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 5
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
Ch.XIII p.145-147 "Prejudice.-Opposed" - "is their due."

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• COMPOSITION - Compare/Contrast
Compare and contrast the similarities and differences between candour and prejudice.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 6
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
Ch.XIII p.147-149 "Discernment.-See," - "works of others."

• How important is discernment in our modern world?  What are some practical steps students can take to develop discernment?

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update character traits chart. Think of a new habit you would like to cultivate and create a tracking chart.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 7
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
Ch.XIV p.150-153 "Truth is not" - "at our hands."

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS: "Calumny of Apelles" by Botticelli
∞ Art Print Link: Calumny of Apelles 

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 8
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
Ch.XIV p.153-155 "Insidiousness and Envy" - "of human nature."

• What are the differences and similarities of a fanatic and a martyr?

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 9
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
Ch.XV p.156-158 "We have not" - "even less accurate."

• Tell about the importance of using veracity when communicating with others. Can you think of any examples from your own life or others?

• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart this week.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 10
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
Ch.XV p.158-160 "Exaggeration-Another" - "win a laugh."

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• COMPOSITION - Story
Write a story of someone who found themselves in trouble due to their habit of exaggeration.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 11
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
Ch.XV p.160-162 "The Realm of" - "the divine seal."

• How does fiction affect your enthusiasms?

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Write a brief reflection on how you did with habit cultivation this term.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 12
lessons.body:
Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course.

Questions will come from:
Ourselves: Book 1

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 13
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ NOTE
Be sure to narrate in a variety of ways.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
Pt.III Ch.XVI p.163-164 "Malicious Lies" - "they think of us."

• Describe some causes of lying. What cause do you most resonate with as a temptation for you?

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update character traits chart. Think of a new habit you would like to cultivate and create a tracking chart.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 14
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS 
Pt.III Ch.XVI p.164-166 "Romancing Lies" - "every valiant Mansoul."

• Is it ever acceptable to lie for a friend's sake? Why or why not? 

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log. Start a chart where you can list desirable human character traits on one side and their daemons on the other.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 15
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Pt.III Ch.XVII p.167-169 "Integrity in Work" - "to the home daughter."

• In what sense are you a "paid labourer"?  What obligation of integrity do you have?

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 16
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Pt.III Ch.XVII p.169-171 "Integrity grows" - "unruly filly, inclination."

• What opportunities have you had to do a job wholly and with integrity.

• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart this week.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 17
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ & NARRATE
Pt.III Ch.XVII p.171-173 "Do the Chief Thing" - "enjoy the day's leisure."

• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 18
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Pt.III Ch.XVII p.174-176 "Cribbin  Time-Some" - "by sins of negligence."

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update character traits chart. Think of a new habit you would like to cultivate and create a tracking chart.

➜ JOURNAL (with remaining time)
Write about an opportunity you have to be honest in material things.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 19
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ & NARRATE
Pt.III Ch.XVII p.176-178 "Bargains-There is" - "of us a hearing."

• Write or tell about the importance of returning borrowed property.  Have you had an experience where something of yours was not returned promptly?  How did you feel about it?

• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 20
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Pt.III Ch.XVIII p.179-181 "Three 'Opinions'-" - "before we utter it."

• What makes an opinion worth having? 

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 21
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Pt.III Ch.XVIII p.181-183 "Fads-But such" - "to give opinions."

• What are the benefits and dangers of being a faddist? Give an example that you've seen in real life if you can!

• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart this week.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 22
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ & NARRATE
Pt.III Ch.XVIII p.183-186 "Opinions about" - "to take up."

• Based on your reading, what is your duty with regard to opinion? 

• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Write a brief reflection on how you did with habit cultivation this term.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 23
lessons.body:
Catch-Up Day

➜ CATCH UP
Use this time to catch up on lessons in this or another course, or explore Extra Helpings.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 24
lessons.body:
Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course.

Questions will come from:
Ourselves: Book 1

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 25
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ NOTE
Be sure to narrate in a variety of ways.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Pt.III Ch.XIX p.187-189 "Principles, Bad and" - "thing we like."

• What is the difference between a good and bad principle?

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update character traits chart. Think of a new habit you would like to cultivate and create a tracking chart.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 26
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Pt.III Ch.XIX p.189-190 "Our Principles Writ" - "must bring forth."

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log. Start a chart where you can list desirable human character traits on one side and their daemons on the other.

➜ JOURNAL (begin during lesson and finish on your own time)
Consider one of your principles. Describe it and tell whether it is a principle worth having. Why or why not? If it is worth having, what can you do to further develop this principle in your life? If not, what should you focus upon instead?

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 27
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Pt.III Ch.XX p.191-193 "My Duty to" - "it may be."

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 28
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Pt.III Ch.XX p.193-195 "Stubbornness does not" - "a sound brain."

• Write or tell about the dangers of self-indulgence. Have you seen this in your own life of others'?

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 29
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ & NARRATE
Pt.III Ch.XX p.195-197 "The Fate of" - "all the time."

• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• COMPOSITION - Interview
Imagine you are a journalist, interviewing Miss Mason about the importance of self-ordering for young people.  What questions might you ask?  What answers might she give?

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 30
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Pt.III Ch.XX p.198-201 "The Ways of" - "of Grecian noses!"

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update character traits chart. Think of a new habit you would like to cultivate and create a tracking chart.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 31
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Pt.III Ch.XX p.201-203 "Interests in Life" - "has made provision."

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart.
lessons.teacherNotes: • COMPOSITION - Tell of the importance of glorifying God with your thoughts.  You may write this composition as a poem.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 32
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ & NARRATE
Pt.IV p.204-206 "Plans-I'm going" - "just, and true."

• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart.
lessons.teacherNotes: • COMPOSITION - Write your answer to Miss Mason's question from p.205 of today's reading, "What sort of person is of use to the world?"  Tell what things you can do to become that sort of person.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 33
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Pt.IV p.206-208 "Possibilities-There is" - "for their chances."

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Update habits log and character traits chart.

➜ JOURNAL
Compare one bad habit you have with the good habit you would like to develop.  Make a realistic plan with two or three small steps to grow in your desired habit.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 34
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Ourselves: Book I

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Pt.IV p.208-210 "Servant of Master" - "meant for thee."

➜ CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Write a brief reflection on how you did with habit cultivation this term.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• ILLUSTRATE
Create a visual showing some of the ways that a person can prepare body, mind, soul, and heart for their calling.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 35
lessons.body:
Catch-Up Day

➜ CATCH UP
Use this time to catch up on lessons in this or another course, or explore Extra Helpings.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 3 - Lesson 36
lessons.body:
Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course.

Questions will come from;
Ourselves: Book 1

terms.content: · Ourselves: Book I: Tell about the functions of love and justice OR Tell of the importance of candour.
· Ourselves: Book I: Tell of some causes of lying OR Tell of the necessity of integrity in the use of time.
· Ourselves: Book I: Tell of justice to yourself, self-ordering OR Tell how you can prepare yourself for your calling, even if you do not know what your calling is.
